speaker: interviewer
text: Rancho Mirage. Interviewer is Rene Firestone.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you, when you first went back to Holland, and you very first time met your friend, describe that meeting.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGH] I had to tell his parents, of course. And him, he was the same age as me. He was three months older than me. He was born 17 December. I'm born the following March. I had to tell them what happened to me, because they said, where's your-- [PAUSES] so I had to tell them my sister, about my mother, about my aunt, my cousin John. I had to tell my friend, Jopi's parents, why it was only me arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told them, of course. And I also was pleased to tell them that they had told me the morning I arrived in Amsterdam that my mother was indeed alive in Sweden. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as my [PAUSES] being for the first time with my friend Jopi, [PAUSES] it was like I'd been away, I don't know, somewhere else. That he hadn't seen me for a while, which in actual fact was two years. [PAUSES] [SIGH] I suppose the feeling was, well, we'll just carry on like we did before, as if nothing was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after you told him, what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I can remember that he put his arms around me, that he actually physically put his arms around me. And, like I said-- obviously, only in Dutch, when he spoke this-- that, you know, he was sorry, what had happened. But basically, that's really all. I can't remember that it was much more. But then he, too, was only, like, 13, 14 years old. [PAUSES] And to his experience, he had also suffered because of the German occupation, as indeed his father and mother-- no food. You know, so it was just another side to the suffering, mine to his. Not more, not less at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came back the second time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back the second time, when I left school in England because I wanted to be back, and then my mother came out. By that time, my aunt came back, my Auntie Annie, and my cousin John. [PAUSES] We were then given an apartment by the [PAUSES] city council of Amsterdam, near to where we had lived before we were put in the ghetto area. We were given an apartment for my mother and myself, my Auntie Annie and Johnny. We lived in that apartment, in the next street, as a matter of fact. [PAUSES] I then, of course, [PAUSES] reestablished my friendship on a-- in a proper way with my friend, boyfriend, who lived around the corner. [PAUSES] And my mother, at that time, I think her eldest brother may have asked my mother what she needed. And I don't know how it came about. I can't really recall. But I know that two bicycles arrived from England, one for my friend Jopi, and one for me-- for me, because a bicycle was like, you know, something terrific, especially after the war. There were no bicycles. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't want to go back to school. My mother said to me, you should go back to school. Do you want to go back to school? I said, no. I don't want to go to school. I don't want to go back to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin John, in 1945, was six. He had to go to school. So he went to the little school, like children do in Holland. My aunt stayed home in the flat. And my mother got a job in Amsterdam as a milliner designer, because that is what she had done in England after her father died when she was young. She had to leave her studies. She did that, and before she got married to my father, lived in Holland, and was a housewife. So my mother did that. And I think we lived rent-free in that apartment, [PAUSES] supplied by the city hall of Amsterdam. [PAUSES] I didn't know what I wanted to do. [PAUSES] Jopi's parents, his father, suggested would I like to become a silversmith, [PAUSES] which is very particular in Amsterdam. [PAUSES] I-- so he took me to a friend of his. And he said, this man will give you an apprenticeship free. Those days, to be an apprentice, you had to pay. And I had nothing. We had nothing. [PAUSES] So after I met the man and I went to his-- we call it in Dutch, like the French, it was 'atelier.' [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't that interested. So Jopi's father-- I used to call him [INAUDIBLE], which is a shortening from-- for [? Jacobus. ?] [PAUSES] He said, would you like to learn the diamond business from the very bottom up? That's what your father was in, your grandfather. Why don't you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't know if I want to do that. He said, well, he said, maybe I could, through this silversmith, get you an apprenticeship free. [PAUSES] This silversmith, with Jopi's father, went to the bourse, the diamond bourse in Amsterdam. [PAUSES] And there were a few Jewish men there, diamond business, either from Belgium or that had come back-- three came back. Three or four came back. And they asked whether they would take me, as a survivor of a [INAUDIBLE] would teach me the business. And they agreed to take me free.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was the first Jewish child in Holland after the war that went back as an apprentice in the diamond industry. [PAUSES] So to start learning in the diamond business, you have to learn it from the bottom. And to start, you have to learn how the diamonds are polished. [PAUSES] So I had to go to this place-- the building is still there, I was there last April-- [PAUSES] to learn the art of polishing diamonds, and to learn where do the diamonds come from, what kind of diamonds come from there, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember how long I was there, but I hated it. And the hate I had was to be confined every day, five days a week, in a place where I couldn't leave. And the only time I could leave was lunch time. [PAUSES] But apparently, I was quite good at what I learned. [PAUSES] And the man that taught me, or started to teach me, he was a non-Jewish man. And his name was Mr. Fandoverbolder. That was his name. That's a real Dutch name. [PAUSES] And the person who was in charge, the manager, was a man called Sam Dresden. [PAUSES] He was a Jewish man who was married to a non-Jewish woman. And in Holland, if you were married to a non-Jewish wife, you were not deported to the east. He was spared. So that man was alive after the war. But I didn't like being inside. And I decided, not with my mother's help. I think I was, you know, hard-headed. I didn't want to do that anymore. And I wanted to go to England. I didn't want to stay in Holland anymore. Too many memories. I couldn't walk down the streets where my aunts and uncles had lived, where my grandparents had lived. I just-- I wanted to be a million miles away from there. [SIGH] I went to England, first alone. [PAUSES] Back to my great-grandmother's house. [PAUSES] And the family, as such, when they started getting demobilized out of the British forces-- my uncles, my two uncles, my mother's eldest brother, my mother's youngest brother, and other uncles. You know, they have me on their hands, 14 years old, very combative. [PAUSES] Don't tell me what to do. Where I've been, you wouldn't believe. You wouldn't dream of where I've been.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you going to do? You have to work. You either go to school, or you work. And to work, you must learn to do something. If you don't want to go to school to study, you must learn to do something. So my mother's eldest brother, and indeed, her youngest brothers, had been beauticians. [PAUSES] So my uncle, the oldest one, Sam, said to me, do you want to learn my business? [PAUSES] So I said, all right. I didn't want to do nothing. I didn't want to learn nothing. [PAUSES] So I was apprenticed to learn that business [PAUSES] in England. But because of my differences with my uncle and my differences with my mother's aunt, Auntie [? Ray, ?] because nobody told me anything, I went to live with another uncle [PAUSES] in another part of London. By that time, my mother had left Holland. So had my aunt [? Anne. ?] So had my cousin, of course, and had come to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a cousin who lived in Johannesburg, South Africa, [PAUSES] a first cousin of my-- both my parents, because they were cousins to each other. And he came to England to see us, to see my mother, me. And he tried to get us to go to South Africa. My mother didn't want to leave her grandmother, her sister, and her sister [? Anne, ?] and her brothers. So they decided not to go to South Africa.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that uncle from South Africa gave my mother some money. [PAUSES] And my mother's eldest brother found my mother an apartment where to live. I then lived with my mother in the apartment, and my mother went to work-- as a milliner, again-- [PAUSES] while I was being apprenticed in the beauty business. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: The law in England before World War II was that if a woman married a foreigner, she lost her nationality, according to British law. The Germans never knew that. But when my mother came back, and wanted to remain in England, she had to reapply for her British citizenship back. And after the war, they changed the law, because so many English-born women had been caught in Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they changed the law in England, whereby she could regain her British nationality. And because I was under 18, I had the choice of either remaining Dutch national, or becoming British. So I chose to be British. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: The unfortunate thing, which I didn't realize at the time, [PAUSES] that at that time, around about 1948, '49, Britain had conscription. [PAUSES] That meant, and would've meant, that I would have had to go into the military in England. And there was no way I was going to be taught how to kill people. And I, really-- and my mother didn't know what I was to do, because I wasn't going to do that. Now, I could've become maybe a conscientious objector, but I didn't know that at the time. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: So someone told me that if I go into the Merchant Navy, the British Merchant Navy, the time I would do there would count against me having to do national service. [PAUSES] So I had the opportunity to be accepted by a company in London who ran all the beauty ship-- beauty shops on passenger ships. And the passenger ships come under the Merchant Navy. So that is what I did. [PAUSES] And thereby, I was allowed leave not to have to go into the military as such. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: You can imagine how my mother felt. I was 17 and a half years old [PAUSES] when I left my mother to go and do that. And I was away, at times, for three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like where? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Australia, [PAUSES] the Far East, the eastern seaboard, the Caribbean, South America. I did this for nearly six years. [PAUSES] So for six years, I was free. [PAUSES] I didn't have to go into the military. I was being paid. I was having good food. And I was in warm weather, sunshine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not married. I was not married. I started as I was 17 and a half years old, and stopped at the end of 1955, approximately, when my mother was taken sick in England with tuberculosis. My mother, at that time, had remarried my stepfather. She remarried him, I think, in 1950-- she married him in 1953 after we got evidence from the Dutch government that my father had died, either in the camp Oranienburg Sachsenhausen, which is near Berlin, or possibly in Belsen. But we don't know the place nor the exact date. My mother then got married to my stepfather, Charles Nathan. [PAUSES] No, I wasn't married at that time. But I stopped doing that because my mother was sick. And my stepfather drove me a bit crazy to get a normal life-- marry a-- find a nice Jewish girl, get married, have a business, all those things. And I think it was influential in my decision to leave what I was doing and to try and lead a normal life. I've yet to need a-- I've yet to lead a normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a job as a beautician [PAUSES] in London, although my mother and stepfather lived on the coast of England. It was about an hour's train journey. I used to travel up by train every day, six days a week. [PAUSES] I worked what was then a very nice, good area in London, in a very well-known place. [PAUSES] And then in 1958, [PAUSES] my uncle came over again from South Africa to visit. He had a brother in England. He had his father and mother still in England, all family. [PAUSES] And he paid us a visit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, what do you want to be here for? He said, it's cold. England's finished. You're too far in Europe. It's no good. It's dangerous. It'll never be any good in Europe. Why don't you come to South Africa? [PAUSES] And I suppose, in a way, I said to myself, why not? I've always been very geographical in my interests. I've always traveled. As a child, I was schlepped backwards and forwards. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to South Africa. As a matter of fact, he got me a job in South Africa, as a beautician, in a-- in the top place in Johannesburg, where he lived. For a while I lived with them, with his wife and his two boys. And then I got a place of my own. And South Africa was a wonderful place in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you date?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in South Africa, [PAUSES] women who I used to attend upon always had either daughters or they had nieces or I know some-- [PAUSES] all that's what you call the Yiddishe business. And I can't bear that. I really cannot bear that. I've never been able to bear that, because indeed, when I lived in England, my stepfather used to say, oh, I do business with a man. He's got-- I'm going to get you-- always. And I-- I used to cringe from all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, I was at my uncle. His name was also Sam Rabbe-- all the same family. I was at his house, and he had invited over a business colleague of his, with his wife and a daughter. [PAUSES] You know, and she wasn't-- she wasn't bad, sort of thing. And by that time-- let me think. In 1958, I was [PAUSES] 26. [PAUSES] I'd learnt a little bit about the opposite sex by then, [PAUSES] maybe more than was good for me. [PAUSES] And I met this girl who had just come out of university in Johannesburg, lived outside Johannesburg in a small town. [PAUSES] And what may seem strange, the father was-- in America, you would call a rancher. In English, you would call a farmer. And strangely enough, there are a lot-- the biggest farms in South Africa were Jewish people, people whose parents had come from Eastern Europe. [PAUSES] Anyway, we started dating, the girl and I. She was educated, which I was not, formally. I was not educated. We started dating. I got on with her well. And I used to go to the farm, although they didn't actually live on the farm. They lived near the farm. And I liked the outdoors. I liked-- and I thought, you know, I love this life here, and I could make a life here. I could make a Jewish life, but not over-Jewish. [PAUSES] I'm a long way away from Europe, which is dangerous, as my mother always tells me. Even today she says to me, no good in Europe. No good in Europe, no good in Europe, will never be any good in Europe, and never be-- I felt I could start a whole new beginning, [PAUSES] far away in miles, far away in memories-- so I thought-- in this sunny country where it's wonderful, if you're white. [PAUSES] That's something I started to realize not long after I started to really live there. And eventually, I gave up being in the beautician business, and I went to work for my father-in-law. [PAUSES] And every day--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married 1960. My mother and stepfather came for the wedding. Relatives that lived in South Africa obviously came to the wedding. And it was a big Jewish do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have one daughter with my wife, my ex-wife. [PAUSES] She is named Helen, after my late sister. Her second name is Isabelle, named after the abbreviation, the Dutch abbreviation, for my father's name. My father's name was Isaac, and in Holland, they would call a person that was called Isaac, [? Ise. ?] So I use-- used [? Ise ?] and made it into Isabelle for a girl. She was born in South Africa. Yes. She's now 34 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in South Africa approximately, I think, [PAUSES] about-- nearly seven years. Seven years, [PAUSES] in which the time I spent on the farms, I loved every minute of it. That was the most wonderful time for me. After my seagoing life, that was the most wonderful time. I was free. I was outside in the warm weather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left because, bottom line, my wife and I got divorced. [PAUSES] My wife and I got divorced. I was getting very much nervous about the situation in South Africa, with the apartheid. My mother kept writing to me, saying to me, it's no good to be there. It's too dangerous. It'll never last there. I could see it could never last. And I wanted to leave. I was afraid. I was afraid to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: With-- with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back-- yes. I went back to England with my wife and my daughter. And my wife hated it in England. She hated the cold. She hated the lack of servants. [PAUSES] She couldn't-- didn't know where the washing machine was, sort of thing, if you know what I mean. She hated it in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- unfortunately, from her side, to be fair on her-- was getting bombarded by letters from her parents. What do you want to do there? It's freezing cold. That's not the life you're used to. You're not born to that. Come back here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, that eventually pulled her back to South Africa, with me following, trying to make another kind of reconciliation with the family. Part of the reason of the divorce, in my opinion, was that I had too much interference-- again, principally from my mother-in-law. You know, God bless them all, but a Jewish mother-in-law isn't too easy to handle, whether you're a boy or a girl. [PAUSES] I must just tell you this. It was so devastating to me. I'd gone back into the beauty business, because I thought that would help, if I disassociated myself business-wise from the family. [PAUSES] And obviously, they had made their minds up to get-- to separate us. I had a phone call from my neighbor at work, and she said, Maurice, you better come home to your apartment because there's a big removal truck outside, and your father-in-law and your wife are emptying your apartment. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked my boss at the time, can I go? I went home. When I got home, to where I'd lived with my wife and my daughter, [PAUSES] the whole apartment had been emptied, and the only thing that remained was one bed and my suitcase with some clothes. That's the only thing that remained.

speaker: interviewee
text: That had had such an effect upon me, even today. When I think, these are my people. My people have done that to me, not Germans. [PAUSES] My people, who know about my past. [PAUSES] It's been a very bad thing for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much longer did you remain in South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I got hold of my uncle who was there. He said come to us, come home straight away. I was there, and I think it must've been maybe a few months. I could--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- let me think. [PAUSES] 30, 32. About 32.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1979. I came to the United States in 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a visit. On a visit to the west coast, to the western part of America. I'd been to the eastern part of America when I was doing my work on ships. I'd known all that part. But my mother had a cousin living here. And I came to visit her, because at that time, I'd had another trauma in my life, because when I went back to England [PAUSES] and my wife stayed in South Africa and my daughter stayed in South Africa. I lived in England, [PAUSES] and I bought a beauty business in England. [PAUSES] And-- [PAUSES] you know, I'm a sucker when it comes to ladies. I fell in love with a beautiful girl whom I married, a non-Jewish girl. She changed by the [INAUDIBLE] in England. My stepfather and my mother were agreeable to it. She came from a very good family. Very English, a very lovely girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to again change tapes. [LAUGHS]
